title: Quantum repeater segment with free-space coupled co-trapped ions using telecom photon interference
authors: Max Bergerhoff, Pascal Baumgart, Christian Haen, Jonas Meiers, Tobias Bauer, Jonas Haferkamp, Christoph Becher, Jürgen Eschner
abstract: A quantum repeater segment is a basic building block of a quantum repeater, generating buffered entanglement of quantum memories to connect quantum repeater cells. It also enables the connection between quantum computers. In the implementation we present here, photons emitted from two co-trapped free-space coupled $^{40}$Ca$^+$ ions are converted to the telecom-C band and interfered after transmission over 440$\,$m of optical fiber (220$\,$m per arm), where a photonic Bell measurement is performed to create entanglement between the memories. With this scheme we generate an entangled $\left|Ψ^+\right\rangle$ Bell state with $\ge 68(8)\,$% fidelity, highlighting trapped $^{40}$Ca$^+$ ions as a promising quantum repeater hardware platform.
summary: The research demonstrates the feasibility of building a quantum repeater segment using co-trapped $^{40}	ext{Ca}^+$ ions. By converting emitted photons to the telecom band and performing interference over long optical fibers, they successfully generated entangled Bell states with high fidelity. This work establishes trapped ions as a promising hardware platform for scalable quantum networks.
main_problem: The primary goal is to implement a quantum repeater segment capable of generating buffered entanglement between distant quantum memories, thereby enabling long-distance quantum communication.
main_result: The authors successfully generated an entangled Bell state with fidelity $\ge 68(8)\%$ using co-trapped $^{40}	ext{Ca}^+$ ions and demonstrated the feasibility of this platform for quantum networking.
method: Entanglement is established by generating photons from two trapped ions, converting these photons to the telecom C band, transmitting them over optical fiber, and performing a photonic Bell State Measurement (BSM).
model_or_system: The physical system utilizes co-trapped $^{40}	ext{Ca}^+$ ions in a Paul trap. The process involves Raman photon generation, quantum frequency conversion (QFC) to 1550 nm, and subsequent entanglement swapping via photonic interference.
key_observables: Entanglement fidelity ($\ge 68(8)\%$), Hong–Ou–Mandel (HOM) visibility ($>0.7$ for $T < 120 	ext{ ns}$), CHSH parameters ($2.42-2.49$), and parity oscillation amplitude ($A = 1.36(15)$).
important_parameters: Fiber length of $440 	ext{ m}$, coincidence time windows $< 120 	ext{ ns}$, and the telecom C band ($\sim 1550 	ext{ nm}$).
main_assumptions: The high fidelity relies on maintaining short coincidence windows for maximum HOM visibility, and future work requires integrating additional trapping nodes or cavities.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the setup schematic (Paul trap, QFCs, BSM), show HOM visibility vs. time window size $T$, and plot parity oscillations versus phase to verify entanglement.
paper_structure: The paper details the experimental realization of the quantum repeater segment protocol, covering photon generation, frequency conversion, long-distance transmission, Bell state measurement, and subsequent characterization using multiple diagnostic tools like HOM interference and parity measurements.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses a core challenge in quantum networking—the distribution of entanglement over lossy channels—by demonstrating a robust hardware platform (trapped ions) compatible with existing telecom infrastructure.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Schematic of the experimental setup, comprising two co-trapped ions in a Paul trap as quantum memories, individual single-photon collection (green), two QFCs (orange), and Bell state analyzer (violet). (b) Level scheme, transitions, and wavelengths of the 40Ca+ ion. (c) Zeeman sub-levels for generation of atom-photon entanglement, showing excitation (blue) and emission paths (red). The relevant levels for generation of the target state (Equation 2) are shown in black. Excitation of the initial state S1/2, −1/2 to P1/2, −1/2 with π-polarized 393-nm light triggers decay to a superposition of |−⟩= D5/2, −3/2

FIG. 2. HOM visibility V (T) plotted as a function of the co- incidence window size T, changed in steps of 1 ns. Measured data points are shown in black, with their associated uncer- tainties represented by shaded regions. In blue, the number of contributing counts is shown. The inset displays the his- tograms of coincidence detections as a function of the detec- tion time difference τ for projections onto orthogonal (yellow bars) and parallel (violet bars) polarizations, evaluated with a bin size of 2 ns. As a guide to the eye, the lines of the cor- responding colors show simulations obtained with the model of [40] and using the experimentally determined parameters.

FIG. 3. Schematic of the sequence for verification of atom-atom entanglement. Generation of atom-photon entanglement. Population transfer from D5/2 to S1/2. Basis rotation by the use of two π/2 RF pulses. Population transfer back from S1/2 to D5/2 followed by state readout.

FIG. 4. Parity as a function of the phase of second π/2 pulse. Black points show the measured data at 0◦, 45◦, 90◦, 135◦; the red line is a sinusoidal fit with its uncertainty indicated by the red shading.

FIG. 5. One of the fifteen peaks in the single-photon wave packets from both ions for one selected detector to measure the time difference at the FBS with the fit as dashed line. Photon 1 (2) refers to the photon emitted by Ion 1 (2).
